story:
Braemore lifts into the smoky sky in his camo quadpod, another run of food and meds bound for the camps west of the big river. He pushes the throttle to ninety-eight, drops to treetop level, and cuts south in a hard, unplanned turn that pins him into the seat. He can’t afford a sentry scrambler of his own, so he flies his aging quad to the edge—enough to keep his adrenaline up and his enemies guessing. He’s wanted by threadbare agencies, double-crossed pirates, and bounty hunters, yet he has dodged them all since joining the ragtag Continentals.

“Hell of a turn, boss,” says Brionna, his co-pilot, twenty-three, bootstrapped out of the Lowland rainforest. She’s been with him since the last Southern Pact surge, back when skies were still blue. She teases his beard—ghastly bird’s nest—then clamps into her seat as sensor blips appear.

A silver Southern Pact scrambler eases up on the port side; another slides in above. “New offensive,” Braemore says. “Dropships and scramblers stacked to the coast.” Brionna only wants out. “Get us into orbit. I’ll hop a star freighter. Anywhere. This planet’s hopeless.” He hums a few bars of his old Oceanic Alliance battle hymn. She burns holes through the control panel with her glare. He’s changed the flight plan again without asking.

“We’re smugglers,” he says. “Today’s enemy is tomorrow’s customer. They want cargo aboard. I’ve cut a very good deal.” She eyes the port window. “New V-2000. Two-seater. Trans-lunar package. What a beast.” He flips a distress signal. “Copy that, Tango One. Onboard emergency. Must land immediately.” She says nothing; she knows his tricks. Below, only charred trunks where oaks and birches used to be.

He aims for a plume of yellow smoke—the agreed landing zone. The upper scrambler combat-lands first, retro exhaust rolling dust across the quad’s windshield. Braemore spins, drops, and kisses the ground. Showoff. Reputation to maintain.

On the ash-gray earth, he studies Brionna’s tired face. “You look as exhausted as I feel.” He hugs her, then pulls an interstellar ID from his coat. “This is for you, Brionna. Or should I say, Henrietta Belmonte?” Tears well. Henrietta was her best friend—the one who didn’t make it when Braemore airlifted their village under a chemical attack. Of the twenty who boarded that day, only Brionna survived.

“It was only a matter of time before these guys or worse caught up to me,” he says, voice unsteady. “I’ve had a good ride. You have your future.” A flash blooms in the western sky, a nuclear blast washing the dead forest in white. The war is just getting started.

“This is why you have to go,” he whispers. “I’m the cargo. I traded myself—and the flight codes to the quadpod, not the quadpod itself— for your freedom.” She refuses, again and again. He points to the V-2000. “Back seat’s yours. Take the new identity. Ride into orbit. Star freighters are waiting. Anywhere. A life with hope.” She clings to him, begs to run. A surly Southern Pact crew member tears her away. She curses—not at Braemore, but at the forces that wrecked her world.

The scrambler knifes upward. Through yellow smoke and gray cloud, Brionna watches him vanish. To this day, betting on Braemore’s slyness, she swears she saw a lone quadpod burst from the clearing and skim west over the blackened trees at full speed, setting a course toward hope, like countless times before.
